---
title: "Measuring Social Inequalities in Health"
subtitle: "Advanced Social Epidemiology PhD Course <br> University of Copenhagen </br>"
author: "Sam Harper"
date: 2026-06-10
date-format: iso
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: [default, ../custom.scss]
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    slide-number: true
    html-math-method: mathjax
    embed-resources: true
    auto-stretch: false
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-image: "../../images/mcgill-epi-logo.png"
    data-background-size: 30%
    data-background-position: 50% 90%
editor: source
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(here)
library(tidyverse)
library(ggplot2)
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE,
  fig.align = "center",
  out.width = "100%"
)
```

## Overview {.h2center}

<br>

### [Conceptual Issues]{.black}

<br>

### [Absolute and Relative Inequality]{.black}

<br>

### [Simple vs. Complex Measures]{.black}

## Overview {.h2center}

<br>

### [Conceptual Issues]{.black}

<br>

### [Absolute and Relative Inequality]{.gray}

<br>

### [Simple vs. Complex Measures]{.gray}

## Why monitor health inequalities?

:::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
<br>

### Surveillance

-   Natural complement to monitoring overall health

-   Essential for detecting important changes in risk

-   Identifying new and emerging health inequalities
:::

::: {.column width = "5%"}
::::

::: {.column width="45%"}
<br>

### Impact

-   Opportunity to evaluate etiological explanations for health inequalities

-   Evaluating the distributional impacts of public health interventions and medical innovations

-   Crucial for measuring the responsiveness of health care systems to those most in need
:::

:::

## Inequalities in health are based on *observations*

<br>

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
### [We are relatively good at measuring inequalities.]{.red}

-   Poor people die younger than rich people

-   Low social class infants have lower birth weight

-   Smokers get more lung cancer than non-smokers

-   Women live longer than men
:::
:::::

## Inequities in health are based on *ethical judgements*

<br>

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
### [Inequities are much harder to measure]{.red}

-   Should poor people die younger than rich people?

-   Should low social class infants have lower birth weight?

-   Should smokers get more lung cancer than non-smokers?

-   Should women live longer than men?
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::: footnote
Adapted from @McGuire2006
:::

:::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Anatomy of an Inequality

-   How much of inequality is *unfair*?

-   How would you know?
:::

:::: {.column width="75%"}
::: r-stretch
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "mcguire-inequity.png"))
```
:::
::::
::::::

## Inequality is an ambiguous concept

Different measures of inequality emphasize different concepts.

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
> "If a concept has some basic ambiguity, then a precise representation of that ambiguous concept must preserve that ambiguity…This issue is quite central to the need for descriptive accuracy in inequality measurement, which has to be distinguished from fully ranked, unambiguous assertions."

> Amartya Sen, *On Economic Inequality*, 1997
:::
:::::

## What should we measure?

::: pull-left
<br>

### Total Health Inequality

-   complement to measurement of average health
-   measured across all individuals
-   avoids normative choice of social groups
-   facilitates unambiguous comparisons over time/place
:::

::: pull-right
<br>

### Social Group Differences in Health

-   measured across normatively important social groups
-   particular social groups chosen a priori
-   provide insights into causal processes linking health and social position
:::

## 

::: footnote
@Asada2002
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Health Inequality Between Whom?

-   Which society has more inequality?

-   Which one is worse from the perspective of inequality?
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images",  "asada-distribution.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 

::: footnote
Source: @harper2017
:::

```{r, out.width="70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", 
  "mse2-fig1b.png"))
```

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Conceptual Issues]{.gray}

<br>

### [Absolute and Relative Inequality]{.black}

<br>

### [Simple vs. Complex Measures]{.gray}

## 

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Easy case

Evidence of clear progress
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Trends in infant mortality, Brazil and Colombia, 1980-2006

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "bra-col-trends.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Easy case

Evidence of clear progress
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Trends in infant mortality, Brazil and Colombia, 1980-2006

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "bra-col-ineq.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 

::: footnote
Source: US NCI SEER\*Stat Database
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Harder case

Are black-white inequalities in prostate cancer mortality increasing or decreasing?
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Black and White Prostate Cancer Mortality Rates

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "bw-prostate-trends.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 

::: footnote
Source: US NCI SEER\*Stat Database
:::

```{r, out.width = "70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "bw-prostate-ineq.png"))
```

## 

::: footnote
@DeLancey2008
:::

```{r, out.width="70%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "delancey-prostate-figure.png"))
```

## Overview {.h2center}

### [Conceptual Issues]{.gray}

<br>

### [Absolute and Relative Inequality]{.gray}

<br>

### [Simple vs. Complex Measures]{.black}

## 

::: footnote
Source: Data2010
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Pairwise comparisons work well for a few groups
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "two-groups.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 

::: footnote
Source: @harper2017
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Somewhat more challenging
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", 
  "mse2-fig2.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Additional groups make summary measures appealing

::: footnote
Source: @persmark2019
:::

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "persmark-2020-ann.png"))
```

## 

::: footnote
Ezzati et al. (2008)
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Summary measures definitely needed

-   Life expectancy inequality across \~3,000 US counties
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "ezzati-counties.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Range-type measures: ignore the entire distribution

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
-   Does A or B have 'more' inequality?

-   Do you have a preference for A or B?
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "range-measure.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 

::: left-column
### Is the amount of inequality the same in these two societies?
:::

::: right-column
```{r, echo=F, out.height="80%", out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "three-groups.png"))
```
:::

## 

```{r, out.width = "90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "ess-education.png"))
```

## 

:::: columns
::: {.column width="20%"}
### Moving beyond simple comparisons

-   More complex measures look at the entire distribution.

-   E.g., Lorenz curve for income, health, or any X:
:::

::: {.column width = "5%"}
::::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Relative Concentration Curve

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "lorenz.png"))
```
:::

:::

## Moving Beyond Binary Comparisons

<br>

### Rank each education group by position in the population distribution

<br>

Distribution of Socioeconomic Position in a Hypothetical Population

| Education Level  |    \% | Cumulative % |     Range     | Midpoint |
|:-----------------|------:|:------------:|:-------------:|---------:|
| None             | 11.93 |    11.93     | 00.00 – 11.93 |     5.97 |
| \<Primary school | 15.04 |    26.97     | 11.93 – 26.97 |    19.45 |
| Primary school   | 26.86 |    53.83     | 26.97 – 53.83 |    40.40 |
| Secondary school | 16.05 |    69.88     | 53.83 – 69.88 |    61.86 |
| Beyond Secondary | 30.12 |     100      | 69.88 – 100.0 |    84.94 |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Summarizing across SEP

-   First rank the population by distribution of SEP

-   Then count up the proportion of disease each group accounts for.
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Relative Concentration Curve

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "cc-cumulative.png"))
```
:::
:::::

------------------------------------------------------------------------

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Summarizing across SEP

-   Diagonal = no inequality

-   Curve above diagonal: ill-health concentrated among poorer.

-   Curve below diagonal: ill-health concentrated among richer.
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Relative Concentration Curve

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "cc-both.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## 

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Summarizing across SEP

-   Concentration Index measures the extent to which disease is 'concentrated' among different SEP groups.
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
Concentration Index

```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "cc-index.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Formula for writing the Concentration Index

::: footnote
See @kakwani1997
:::

::: pull-left
<br>

One way of writing the CI is: $$RCI= \frac{2}{n\mu} \sum_{i=1}^{n}y_{i}R_{i}-1$$ where $\mu$ is the mean of $y_{i}$ (e.g., smoking status), $R_{i}$ is the fractional rank of the *i*th person in the socioeconomic (i.e., income) distribution.
:::

::: pull-right
<br>

The Absolute Concentration Index multiplies *RCI* by the mean smoking rate: $$ACI=\mu*RCI$$
:::

## Bounds of the Concentration Index

::: footnote
@wagstaff2005; @erreygers2009; @wagstaff2009
:::

::: pull-left
<br>

For a **continuous** outcome the RCI is bounded $[-1, 1]$, but for a **binary** outcome (e.g., smoking, disease) the bounds depend on the mean $\mu$:

$$-(1-\mu) \leq RCI \leq \mu$$

E.g., an outcome with a mean of 15%, the maximum *pro-rich* concentration is only $+0.15$, not $+1$.

Makes comparisons across outcomes or populations misleading.
:::

:::: pull-right
::: fragment
<br>

**Wagstaff normalisation** rescales the index to restore $[-1, 1]$ bounds:

$$CI_W = \frac{RCI}{1 - \mu}$$

**Note:** The correction is contested. @erreygers2009 proposed an alternative absolute index $E = 4\mu(1-\mu) \times CI$ with different normative properties; the choice affects interpretation and cross-study comparisons.
:::
::::

## 

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Example of Relative and Absolute CI

1965: Smoking increases with education = + RCI

2003: Smoking decreases with education = - RCI
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "women-smoking-ineq.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Example: Relative CI in health utility across select European countries

::: footnote
Source: @doorslaer2004
:::

```{r, out.width="80%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", 
  "van-doorslaer-ci-europe.png"))
```

## 

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### Slope and Relative Index of Inequality

-   Conceptually similar to CI

-   Correlation between SEP rank and health.

-   SII: Absolute difference

-   RII: Relative difference (or ratio)
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", 
  "sii-graph.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## SII: Calculation

::: pull-left
Regress health outcome on the **midpoint rank** of socioeconomic categories, [weighted]{.red} by population share:

$$y = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\text{Rank}) + \varepsilon$$

$\hat{\beta}_1$ = estimated change in the outcome moving from the **very bottom** (rank = 0) to the **very top** (rank = 1) of the socioeconomic distribution

-   **SII**: absolute change (e.g., percentage points)
-   **RII** $= \hat{\beta}_1 / \bar{y}$: proportional change
-   **RII**$_{KM}$ $= \hat{\alpha} / (\hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta}_1)$: ratio of bottom-to-top (analogous to a risk ratio)
:::

::: pull-right
There is a specific parallel with the RCI by transforming the health variable:

$$2\hat{\sigma}^2_{\text{rank}} \cdot (y/\mu) = \beta_0 + \beta_1(\text{Rank}) + \varepsilon$$

**Relative Concentration Index** $= \hat{\beta}_1$
:::

::: footnote
@kunst1994
:::

## R example: SII

::: footnote
SII $= \hat{\beta}_1$ from weighted OLS of smoking rate on cumulative rank.
:::

::: pull-left
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: false
sii_dat <- tribble(
  ~class,       ~rate,  ~pop,
  "I (low)",    0.32,   170,
  "II",         0.24,   250,
  "III",        0.20,   300,
  "IV",         0.14,   180,
  "V (high)",   0.08,   100
) |>
  mutate(
    cum_pop = cumsum(pop),
    rank = (lag(cum_pop, default = 0) + cum_pop) / 2 / sum(pop)
  )

fit <- lm(rate ~ rank, data = sii_dat, weights = pop)
round(coef(fit), 3)
```

The coefficient on rank is the SII: smoking falls by `r abs(round(coef(fit)["rank"]*100, 1))` percentage points from bottom to top, i.e., when `rank` goes from 0 to 1.
:::

::: pull-right
```{r, fig.width=5.5, fig.height=6, echo=F}
ggplot(sii_dat, aes(rank, rate)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = pop), colour = "#2166ac") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", aes(weight = pop),
              se = TRUE, colour = "#d6604d",
              fill = "#d6604d", alpha = 0.2,
              linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(1),
                     limits = c(0, 0.4)) +
  scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(x = "Rank in cumulative distribution",
       y = "Smoking rate") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) +
  theme(legend.position = "none",
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
```
:::

## Relative Index of Inequality: Example

::: footnote
@kunst1994; @Mackenbach1997
:::

::: pull-left
RII $= \hat{\beta}_1 ;/; \bar{y}$

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: false
mu  <- weighted.mean(sii_dat$rate, sii_dat$pop)
sii <- coef(fit)["rank"]
rii <- sii / mu
round(c(mean = mu, SII = sii, RII = rii), 3)
```

Moving from bottom to top of the SEP distribution, smoking decreases by `r abs(round(rii * 100))`% relative to the mean.
:::

::: pull-right
RII$_{KM}$ [@kunst1994]: $\hat{\alpha} ;/; (\hat{\alpha} + \hat{\beta}_1)$

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| code-line-numbers: false
alpha  <- coef(fit)[["(Intercept)"]]
beta   <- coef(fit)[["rank"]]
rii_km <- alpha / (alpha + beta)
round(c(bottom = alpha,
        top    = alpha + beta,
        RII_KM = rii_km), 3)
```

Smoking at the bottom is `r round(rii_km, 1)` × the rate at the top — analogous to a risk ratio across the full SEP distribution.
:::

## Two mechanisms for changing inequality

::: footnote
@Renard2019
:::

Size of social groups will also change SII/RII without any health change. Increasing the size of higher educated groups (e.g., larger share with higher education) increases inequality:

```{r, out.width="90%"}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "renard-sii.png"))
```

## 

::: footnote
@world2013handbook
:::

::::: columns
::: {.column width="25%"}
### The measure may matter!
:::

::: {.column width="75%"}
```{r}
knitr::include_graphics(here("images", "hosseinpoor-quote.png"))
```
:::
:::::

## Conclusions

Measures of health inequality are not value neutral.

-   Individual vs. social group inequality
-   Absolute vs. relative scales
-   Weighting: how much and to whom?

The choices above have an important impact on our judgments of both the magnitude of health inequality and whether health inequalities are worsening or improving.

Monitoring health inequalities requires both precise measurement and value judgments—they are inseparable.

A suite of health inequality measures is likely necessary to provide a complete description of the magnitude of inequality.

## References

::: {#refs}
:::
